title_en: The Art of Hadith Criticism — Jarh wa Ta'dil
content_en:
Jarh wa ta'dil — the sciences of narrator criticism (disparagement) and attestation (authentication) — is the engine that drives the entire system of hadith authentication. Without reliable tools for evaluating the character and memory of the thousands of individuals who transmitted prophetic narrations across generations, the entire edifice of hadith science would collapse. Ibn al-Salah's treatment of this subject in the Muqaddimah is among its most practically important sections.

The word 'jarh' (disparagement) refers to the identification and articulation of qualities in a narrator that impair their reliability as a transmitter — whether moral failures, memory defects, deliberate deception, excessive error, or other disqualifying traits. The word 'ta'dil' (authentication/attestation) refers to the identification and articulation of qualities that establish a narrator as trustworthy, precise, and acceptable. Together, jarh wa ta'dil constitute the biographical-critical science (rijal criticism) that is unique to Islamic scholarship — nothing comparable existed in any other religious or intellectual tradition before its development by Muslim scholars in the second and third centuries of Islam.

Ibn al-Salah discusses the conditions under which jarh (criticism of a narrator) is permissible and obligatory. Normally, backbiting (ghiba) — speaking of a person's faults in their absence — is prohibited by Islamic law. But the scholars unanimously held that criticizing narrators for the purpose of protecting the integrity of prophetic hadith is an exception: it is not only permissible but obligatory, because the alternative — allowing unreliable narrators to be cited as if reliable — corrupts the religious knowledge of the entire ummah. This principle was applied without social favoritism: even prominent scholars, beloved friends, and relatives of the critics were subjected to the same scrutiny. The famous Imam al-Bukhari criticized his own teacher Ismail ibn Abi Uwais despite his great love and respect for him, because the integrity of the hadith demanded it.

The scholars of jarh wa ta'dil developed a rich vocabulary of evaluative terms, organized in a hierarchy. For attestation (ta'dil), terms range from the highest praise — 'ثقة ثبت' (absolutely trustworthy and firm), applied to the most reliable narrators — down to terms indicating moderate trustworthiness. For criticism (jarh), terms range from the mildest — 'layyin' (slightly soft/unreliable), 'fi hifzihi shay'' (there is something in his memory) — down to the most severe: 'kadhdhaab' (liar), 'wada' al-hadith' (he fabricated hadiths), applied to narrators whose narrations are permanently discarded.

The most distinguished scholars of this science include Yahya ibn Ma'in (158–233 AH), Ali ibn al-Madini (161–234 AH), Ahmad ibn Hanbal (164–241 AH), al-Bukhari (194–256 AH), Abu Zur'ah al-Razi (200–264 AH), Abu Hatim al-Razi (195–277 AH), al-Nasa'i (215–303 AH), and later al-Daraqutni (306–385 AH) and al-Dhahabi (673–748 AH). Their evaluations of individual narrators, preserved in the great biographical dictionaries (kutub al-rijal), remain the primary reference for every hadith scholar seeking to authenticate or criticize a narration.
